sentences.text: They probably got certain orders from the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that certain people have to leave and certain people remain.
Because after we left, there were-- they formed a larger ghetto, and people were confined in a certain part of the city, that they formed a Yiddish ghetto.
But we were not there.
When we arrived to [?
Krupp, ?] we were resettled again from the Judenrat.
And we were given-- as a family, we got an empty room in a certain part of town.
But at that time, it was formed that the whole city had to-- all the Jews-- had to work as [NON-ENGLISH].
And as I said, the part of the town was owned by a Pole who owned a lot of land, a lot of fields, a lot of homes.
And the Germans took it over.
And we were working in the fields for the Germans, as forced labor, into the fields, in all kind of work that they took us every morning to go to work.
Krupp ?] was not a big town.
It was a small town, mostly occupied from Poles.
There were, from before the war, certain Jews, that they lived over there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were all subjected to [NON-ENGLISH], as soon-- as soon as the war broke out.
And we were working in the field, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my brother, my older brother and I, and my father, used to work in the field, but my mother had to tend the little baby.
But many days, she used to work in the office, over there by the Germans, or whatever they told her to do in the fields, and leave my younger brother with the baby, home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [?
Krupp, ?] unfortunately, is a very sad chapter in my life.

Because one of the days, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess the [NON-ENGLISH] got a quarter to deliver the unwanted Jews, unable-- people that were unable to work, old people and children that were unable to contribute to work, or they were unable to work, they went into transport.
We didn't know about it, but one morning, they pulled up a truck in front of our home, where we lived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they tried to take away the two children.
They came to pick up the two small children, because they were unable to contribute to work.
As I said, the baby was born May 1, 1938.
My younger brother from me was born April 18, 1930.
So he was 9 years old or reaching 10 years old.
And they were no good to work.
So they came to pick up the children into transport.
And my mother was at that time 37 years old, and she refused to give up the children.
So when she started to cry and refused to give up the children, the German grabbed her by the hair and threw her in the truck, together with the children.
And the last words that I remember my mother said to me; [NON-ENGLISH], take care of Daddy.
And when the trucks pulled away, we couldn't visualize that I would never see my mother again.
That was the last time I saw my two younger brothers and my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My older brother and I, we continued to work in the fields [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for quite a few months.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we kept on hoping that someday-- my father used to give us hope that someday, when all this would get over, that we will meet again with my mother and the children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we woke up, the whole town was surrounded by not just trucks, but army trucks.
With so many Germans.
We were surrounded.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were taken into ghetto-- into the ghetto Rejowiec.
All mostly Polish.
They used to count us.
While we were working in the field, they were beating us up.
The Poles.
In fact, I was only 13 years old.
And when I was working in the field, and it was-- we used to work with the-- with the-- in the cornfield.
And when I used to go with the pitch-- the pitch fork was bigger than I was at that time.
And the-- the corn, the amount of corn that were picked up, they were so heavy, that I couldn't pick it up.
So the Polish girls, they used to beat us up.
They used to say, who do you think you are?
You Jew, you think you're home?
You spoiled!
You never worked a day in your life!
And they used to taunt us with very dirty language.
But-- Was anybody trying to run away from there?
Did you try and make contact with--
We couldn't run away from there.
I really didn't know at that time.
At that time, I didn't know.
I was too young to realize.
And I really didn't understand of too much what really goes on.
My father used to work in the fields.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We lived with hope.
We didn't know that this nightmare will last six years.
That we would be all separated.
We would lose one another.
But I guess the hope and the dream of being together one day, and all of this nightmare will end, kept us going.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But when we arrived to Rejowiec, with the trucks, it was a ghetto.
And were unprepared.
We were separated.
Men were living in different quarters.
And women were living separate, in different quarters.
And we were working mostly in a sugar factory.
This was end of '41.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were working in the sugar factory.
We were loading up the-- the molasses and the brown sugar and schlepping big sacks of sugar into the warehouses.
And I-- mostly, I was working night shift.
And we used to load up all the kettles with the molasses or the brown sugar.
That used to refine the sugar.
It was a refinery.
Also in the sugar factory.
I did see them.
Yes.
But after work, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we used to have a day shift, we used to march in.
We used to be counted when we used to march in into ghetto.
And then we used to be marched outside together to clear up the ruins from the buildings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One particular day, I was carrying stones from one building to another, and I had to go to the bathroom.
I was hiding in one of the empty buildings, and before I looked around, a German stood over me and hit me over the head.
And in that ghetto, they had the Germans dug a big grave behind the ghetto.
They didn't bury people.
They dug up a big grave that the water came up.
And whoever was shot was near that grave and remained in that grave.
That particular morning, when the-- when the German found me in that empty building, I taught that they were going to shoot me right away.
But he took me to the grave over there, outside the ghetto, and told me to jump in, into the water.
I jumped in the water, and nobody came from that grave out alive.
I jumped into that water, and he was pushing me with the [NON-ENGLISH], with a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a snake.
He had like a leather--
Whip.
And he beat me.
And while I was running from corner to corner.
And I was waiting already to be shot.
And I stumbled over the bodies that they were already in that grave.
And I was drinking the water from it, because I fell into the water.
And when I fell, he was beating me up with the whip.
And finally, another German came over on a motorcycle and was talking to that SS that was beating me up.
I guess my time wasn't up.
All of a sudden, when I was standing to the wall, waiting to be shot, he was screaming, [GERMAN]!
And I jumped out from the pit, and he told me to get back into the ghetto.
When I came back into the ghetto, the Jewish people that I was working with them, that shift, recited the kaddish.
They thought that I would never come out.
And-- because nobody came out alive from that pit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway--
No.
But the next-- that was after the shift.
But I was continuing-- I continued to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a while, we were working there, and the hunger was so great in that ghetto.
We didn't have what to eat.
And one day, they made selections.
They eliminated people.
So they made selections.
They put you in a line, and whoever looked frail, or whatever, not able to work, they took them out from the ghetto and loaded up on a truck.
That means that they went to their death.
No.
At that time, we didn't know.
No.
At that time, we didn't know at all.
No news was coming back.
We thought maybe they go to be resettled somewhere.
We didn't realize at that time so much cruelty.
We knew of beatings.
We knew of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kinds of things.
But we didn't know yet of being gassed or put to death in such a way.
We knew of shooting or beating up, which went on constantly.
But we didn't know yet the real things to what came later.
Rejowiec.
Selections went on constantly.
Because there was-- at a certain time, I had typhoid in that ghetto.
And Germans were terribly afraid when somebody was sick on typhoid.
So they surrounded the buildings.
They surrounded the sick people, and they-- or they shoot them in that pit.
Or they loaded them up, as sick as they were, on trucks, to different pits.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But I-- when I was sick on the typhoid, the leader from the sugar factory that I worked, he told me once that he had a daughter that he lost, my age.
And he kind of felt sorry when I had typhoid, and I couldn't go a few days to work.
When I had crisis, when I had 105 temperature.
That night, he came in, and he said to me, Miriam, tomorrow morning, when it's the appell-- appell means that they counted people in the morning, before we marched out to work.
He says, you must appear tomorrow morning for the counting.
I says, I can't.
I can't stand on my feet.
I was so sick.
He says, we will dress you, and we will put you out to the appell.
You must go tomorrow to work.
But we didn't know why.
The next morning, people came and dressed me.
And we were standing five in a row in the line.
And one was holding my clothes in the back.
One was holding the clothes in front.
And that's the way I went through the counting.
And I marched out that way.
People were holding under my arms.
They took me to the sugar factory.
And that same man put me between sacks of sugar all day, because I couldn't-- I was unable to work.
Yes.
He was from Czechoslovakia.
The man.
In fact, he had a son that was shot by the gate when they-- when they were taking us to-- to Majdanek, because he fell, and he was on crutches.
So he was unable to work.
So they shoot him.
But anyway, I recuperated from the typhoid, and I went back to work.
And one day, we were surrounded-- the whole ghetto was surrounded by army and trucks and SS .
And we were loaded up again into trucks.
And with the trucks, we came to Majdanek.
That's a concentration camp.
Mostly not.
There were none.
If you were sick, you had to disguise that you were well.
You were not allowed to be sick, because there was no hospital, or there was no help for a Jew to get medicine to get better.
In fact, they were standing by the electric wires and taunting us with all the dirty words if they saw us marching, or whatever.
Always.
They spit.
There were.
There were certain people that-- I remember a friend of mine, a woman that worked with me in the sugar factory, tried to escape.
And she looked so Polish.
And she had-- supposedly, she had friends behind the ghetto.
And she thought that she could escape and live by them.
We really couldn't find out if it was set up.
But as soon as she came out from the ghetto, the SS was waiting for her, and they brought her to that pit, and they shot her there.
Yes.
As a punishment to us, to show that we shouldn't escape, they brought us to the pit, and they lined us up.
And they said, you see?
That's what happens if you will escape.
Or they used to-- we used to have a lot of hanging in the ghetto.
They used to hang certain people for not doing their work, or whatever the reason it was.
Women and-- women and men.
And they used to have the hanging, and they used to line us up to show us whatever will happen if we try to do the same.
Two of them.
Yes.
Not that I really know.
One of them stole some bread.
Fight for survival.
You do anything.
And when you are hungry, you don't think of consequences.
You try to save your life.
And he stole some bread.
And he was caught.
And I know that a young boy was hanged, because when we were marching through the gate, he was stopped.
And he had some vegetables that he found in the field.
And he took it out from his shirt, and he was hanged because of that.
At one point, there was a selection.
This was right after typhoid.

And we were afraid-- in fact, that very-- after they massacred, the day when I went to the sugar factory, while I had typhoid, they had a selection while I was away, and they were digging up all the basements, looking for people that they were in hiding.
They shot a lot of people in bed.
The ones that they had typhoid.
And when we came back from the-- from work, we found-- we found a lot of empty homes, that people were not there anymore.
That they were shot.
In fact, the bed that I slept in was overturned, because underneath the bed was a basement.
Not a basement, but it was in the ground, like a-- in Poland, they used to be-- call it as a basement.
Poles used to store food in there.
It was in the ground.
And it was all dug up, looking for me, because they knew there was another victim of typhoid.
So we were afraid.
The people that survived that selection was afraid that will-- that they would come again for us.
So one day, we found out there was going to be another selection.
And that day, I escaped from the room.
And I was hiding.
It's unbelievable when I talk about it.
There was no place where to hide.
And we had outhouses.
There was no bathroom.
We had no bathrooms.
But there was outhouses in the back of the house, with holes made in the wood that we used as outhouses.
And to hide, I jumped into-- inside, and holding onto the board till the selection was over.
And they took out a few thousand Jewish women, women and men at that time, to be killed.
I really don't know what made me do it and how I did it, but there was a river behind.
And when I jumped out from outhouse, I went into that water and washed myself as best as I could.
And that's the way I survived.
It's the instinct that told you what to do and how to do it.
But I was a young girl, and I guess when you are young, you-- it was fight to live.
They were still in the ghetto.
No.
Not at all.
It was a fight for survival.
We were all hungry and dirty.
The rats were eating us up.
And we had, in our clothes-- because we had no means of washing clothes properly-- we had lice that they were so big, that we couldn't even kill them.
That's how big they were.
That we used to take our clothes, when we used to come in from work-- and this was our spare time.
We used to delouse ourselves, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because we were eaten up alive.
And at night, we had rats.
Even if you had a little bit of food or a piece of bread that you saved for next morning, the rats used to eat it up.
So that was our life in the ghetto.
And then it came to an end when we were surrounded.
And it was [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They called it [NON-ENGLISH] because the whole ghetto was transferred to Majdanek.
And they made a cleaning of Jews.
And we arrived to Majdanek in trucks.
So the entire Jewish population--
Population, yes.
We were loaded in trucks, and we came to Majdanek in Lublin.
Majdanek.
When we arrived, it was fall.
And we arrived-- we had only the clothes, whatever we had on our back.
And when we arrived, the [NON-ENGLISH] from Majdanek--
[NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know his name.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was wearing a white coat, white gloves, and a whip in his hand.
And we were taken off from the trucks.
And it was-- we were putting in a line, and we were separated, men from women.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] with the whip, he directed us right or left, left or right.
We really didn't know where we were going.
We were, but we were separated.
They went to the-- to the men's line, and I was to go with the women.
I really don't know how they selected me in the other line.
I didn't know if that line was going to live or that line is going to death.
Because as we entered Majdanek, the stench of human flesh was so great, we couldn't visualize.
Because from far away, when we came to Majdanek, we saw big, tall chimneys.
And the stench was so terrible, like rubber.
We couldn't visualize that this is human flesh.
But it was a terrible odor that we--
No.
We didn't know at that time.
When we were put in line to the selection, the [NON-ENGLISH] selected us to left or right.
And the line that I went in, we were marched-- we-- they took us in, to march into the camp.
The rest of them, we didn't know where they were going.
But later, we found out that they went to the gas chambers.
Yes.
Why did we hear about it?
Because when we were marching into camp, the line that I was selected, we marched into camp, and we were marching in-- under the orders from kapos.
Some of the kapos were Germans, some of the kapos were Jews, with a band wearing on their arm.
And the Jews told us, you're lucky, because you were selected to live.
Because you're going to work.
So we were asking, what do you mean to live?
Where are the other people?
He said, why?
Don't you know where you are?
This is a death camp.
Whoever is not in the line, do you see the chimneys?
Those are-- this is odor from flesh.
From human lives.
What did you think and feel?
We couldn't visualize.
I was a young girl, and I was really a child.
And how was I-- how was I selected to go into the line to be alive?
I really don't know to this day.
Because I was a young girl, and I wasn't even really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pretty, I can't even say.
Because I didn't know at that time if I was a girl or a boy.
Because I wasn't even [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] looking like a woman.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I was taken into the line.
We marched into camp.
And we were going into showers.
And we were disrobed.
And they were throwing to us clothes.
Whatever-- whatever was thrown to you, you had to wear.
And we were wearing certain clothes, and we were painted on our clothes, a big Kalel.
It means [?
Concentrations Lager ?].
In the back, I was painted.
And in the front, I had a painted [NON-ENGLISH].
And we were taken into showers and giving us the clothes.
And then we were taken into barracks.
